_disclaimer: Synthetic starter reference data based on commonly cited general norms in Indian metros. Not legal advice. Verify against local/state tenancy law before relying on this data.

deposit.notes: Some states (e.g. Karnataka draft rules) propose capping deposits at ~2-3 months rent. Deposits above 6 months are uncommon and worth questioning. Non-refundable deposits are a strong red flag.

notice_period.red_flag_asymmetry: If landlord's required notice is much shorter than tenant's (e.g. landlord 15 days vs tenant 60-90 days), this one-sided imbalance is a red flag.
notice_period.notes: 30 days is the most common notice period in standard agreements.

lock_in_period.notes: Lock-in periods exist to protect both parties from short-term churn, but should allow reasonable early exit (e.g. with notice + partial penalty), not outright forfeiture.

rent_increase.red_flag: Any clause allowing the landlord unilateral, unlimited, or no-notice rent increases is a red flag regardless of percentage.
rent_increase.notes: 5-10% annual increase on renewal is common practice.

painting_charges.typical: Landlord bears painting cost before move-in; tenant only pays for damage beyond normal wear and tear.
painting_charges.red_flag: Unconditional/flat painting deduction (fixed months' rent) regardless of actual wall condition is a red flag, as it charges the tenant even if no painting is needed.

maintenance.typical: Landlord bears structural/major repair costs; tenant bears minor day-to-day upkeep.
maintenance.red_flag: Clauses shifting full structural repair liability (regardless of cause) onto the tenant are unusual and worth negotiating.

termination.typical: Either party may terminate with agreed notice; no penalty after lock-in ends.
termination.red_flag: Clauses allowing landlord to terminate/repossess without court process, or enter the property without notice, may conflict with local tenancy protections and are a strong red flag.

utilities.typical: Billed as per actual usage/meter readings.
utilities.red_flag: Flat, non-itemized utility charges set solely at landlord's discretion, with no bills provided, reduce transparency and are worth questioning.
